skill.name: docker-optimizer
skill.description: Reviews Dockerfiles for best practices, security issues, image size, multi-stage builds, cache efficiency, and container deployment readiness.
skill.summary: Audit and improve Dockerfiles for smaller, safer, and more reliable container images.
skill.author: CrazyDubya

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported many shell, filesystem, secret, and blocker patterns, but review found these are Markdown examples inside Dockerfile guidance. No executable scripts, network calls, prompt injection, credential exfiltration, or destructive host operations were found. The skill is low risk because it is community content that can guide file edits, but the reported high-risk patterns are false positives.

low_findings.title: False Positive: Markdown Examples Detected as Commands
low_findings.description: The external command detections are Dockerfile, bash, and inline Markdown examples used to explain container optimization. The skill declares Read, Grep, Glob, Write, and Edit tools, and the reviewed content does not execute these examples itself.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The flagged lines are inside Markdown text or fenced examples, not executable Ruby or shell code in the skill. The allowed tools do not include shell execution.

low_findings.title: False Positive: Docker Layer Cleanup Misread as Destructive Delete
low_findings.description: The recursive delete pattern is an example that removes package manager cache files from a Docker image layer. It targets /var/lib/apt/lists inside a Dockerfile example, not the host root or a user home directory.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The command appears in a Dockerfile code fence as a standard image-size cleanup step. The target path is package-list cache data, not a broad filesystem deletion target.

low_findings.title: False Positive: Secret Examples Are Explicit Anti-Patterns
low_findings.description: The .env and API key detections appear in guidance that tells users to exclude secrets or avoid hardcoded credentials. No real credential value or exfiltration behavior is present.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The surrounding headings label these snippets as .dockerignore guidance and bad secret handling examples. They are instructional content rather than operational secret access.

low_findings.title: False Positive: No Weak Cryptography Evidence
low_findings.description: The weak cryptographic algorithm blocker maps to the skill description line, but that line only describes Dockerfile review for security issues. No cryptographic algorithm or implementation is present there.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Line 3 is plain metadata text and contains no crypto code or algorithm name. The static blocker has no semantic support in the reviewed content.

content.value_statement: Large or unsafe Docker images slow deployments and increase operational risk. This skill reviews Dockerfiles and proposes focused improvements for size, security, caching, and maintainability.
actual_capabilities: Finds Dockerfiles by common names and extensions.
Reviews base image pinning, layer order, and cache efficiency.
Suggests multi-stage build patterns for smaller final images.
Checks for non-root users, health checks, and secret handling risks.
Recommends build commands that use BuildKit and registry cache sources.
limitations: Does not build images or measure actual image sizes by itself.
Does not run vulnerability scanners unless the user runs them separately.
Examples focus mainly on Node.js Dockerfile patterns.
Requires project context to make accurate package and runtime choices.

use_cases.title: Reduce Deployment Image Size
use_cases.description: Review an oversized Dockerfile and propose multi-stage builds, slimmer base images, and cache cleanup steps.

use_cases.title: Improve Container Security
use_cases.description: Check Dockerfiles for root users, hardcoded secrets, missing health checks, and broad file copying.

use_cases.title: Prepare a Service for CI Builds
use_cases.description: Tune Dockerfile layer order and .dockerignore contents so dependency layers rebuild less often.

prompt_templates.title: Review My Dockerfile
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner review of one container definition.
prompt_templates.prompt: Review the Dockerfile in this project. List the main issues by impact, then suggest a safer and smaller version.

prompt_templates.title: Create a .dockerignore
prompt_templates.prompt: Inspect this project and draft a .dockerignore that excludes dependencies, local files, secrets, test artifacts, and documentation not needed in the image.

prompt_templates.title: Design a Multi-Stage Build
prompt_templates.scenario: Convert a single-stage build into a production-focused image.
prompt_templates.prompt: Convert this Dockerfile to a multi-stage build. Keep the final image minimal, run as a non-root user, and preserve runtime behavior.

prompt_templates.title: Audit for Production Readiness
prompt_templates.prompt: Perform a production readiness audit for the Docker setup. Cover security, caching, image size, health checks, build reproducibility, and CI build performance.

output_examples.input: A Dockerfile uses a latest base image, copies the entire repository before installing dependencies, and runs as root.
output: Pin the base image to a specific supported version.
Copy package files before source files so dependency installation can be cached.
Create a dedicated application user and switch to it before runtime.

output_examples.input: A service image includes local dependencies, documentation, and environment files in the build context.
output: Add dependency folders, local environment files, and version control metadata to .dockerignore.
Copy only the files required for install, build, and runtime.
Keep secrets outside the image and pass configuration at runtime.

output_examples.input: A Node.js Dockerfile builds and runs the application in the same image.
output: Use a build stage for dependency installation and compilation.
Copy built assets and production dependencies into a smaller runtime stage.
Add a health check and use a non-root runtime user.

best_practices: Review suggested Dockerfile changes against the actual runtime before applying them.
Pin base image versions and update them through a controlled dependency process.
Run a container scanner after optimization to verify vulnerability changes.
anti_patterns: Do not copy the entire repository into the image before installing dependencies.
Do not bake secrets, local environment files, or private keys into images.
Do not assume Alpine or slim images are compatible without testing native dependencies.

faq.question: Does this skill run Docker builds?
faq.answer: No. It reviews files and suggests commands or edits. The user must run builds separately.

faq.question: Can it reduce every image size?
faq.answer: No. Some images are already minimal, and some applications require large runtime dependencies.

faq.question: Does it support languages beyond Node.js?
faq.answer: Yes, it can review general Dockerfile patterns. Its bundled examples are strongest for Node.js projects.

faq.question: Will it find secrets in my image?
faq.answer: It can flag obvious Dockerfile and .dockerignore risks. It does not inspect built image layers by itself.

faq.question: Can it create a production Dockerfile?
faq.answer: Yes. It can propose a production-focused Dockerfile when it has enough project context.

faq.question: Should I use all suggested changes?
faq.answer: No. Validate each change against your build process, runtime dependencies, and deployment environment.
